coredata.dc:description: The unpredictable release behavior of metal nanoparticles/metal ions from metal nanoparticle-loaded hydrogels, without a suitable in situ detection method, is resulting in serious cytotoxicity. To optimize the preparation and design of antibacterial hydrogels for in situ detection of metal nanoparticles, an in-situ detection platform based on the fluorescence signal change caused by the potential surface energy transfer of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) and carbon dots (CD) through silver mirror reaction and Schiff base reaction was established. The antimicrobial test results show that the composite antimicrobial hydrogel, with lower dosages of AgNPs and CD, exhibited a higher inhibition rate of 99.1 % against E. coli and 99.8 % against S. aureus compared to the single antimicrobial component. This suggests a potential synergistic antimicrobial activity. Furthermore, the fluorescence detection platform was established with a difference of <3 μg between detected values and actual values over a period of 72 h. This demonstrates the excellent in situ detection capability of the hydrogel in antimicrobial-related applications.

A) HRTEM images and D) the lattice fringes of AgNPs. B) TEM images and E) particle size distribution of CD. Cross-sectional SEM images of C) CD/ODEX and F) CD/ODEX@AgNPs (The inserted part was pore size distribution of hydrogels). G) The fluorescence spectra of CD. H) UV–vis absorbance spectra of CD (The inserted part was photographs of CD aqueous solution in sunlight and UV excitation) and I) ODEX/AgNPs (The inserted part was photographs of ODEX/AgNPs aqueous solution in sunlight).

A) XRD, and B) zeta potentials of ODEX and ODEX/AgNPs. C) FT-IR spectrum of the DEX, ODEX, CD, and CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogel. D) XPS spectrum of CD/ODEX, CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogel, and corresponding high-resolution XPS of E, F) C1s, G) N1s, and H) Ag 3d. I) Elemental mapping images of C, N, O, and Ag of CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogel.

B) Photograph of inhibition zone of the CD/ODEX-1 and CD/ODEX@AgNPs-2 hydrogel against E. coli and S. aureus after incubating at 37 °C for 24 h and the result of contacting with CD/ODEX and CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogel discs for 10 min. C) Inhibition zone of the control group and CD/ODEX and CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogel against E.coli and S. aureus after incubating at 37 °C for 24 h and D) the result of contacting with ODEX (8 μg/mL), CD (8 μg/mL), AgNO3(Ag+: 8 μg/mL), ODEX/AgNPs(AgNPs: 8 μg/mL), CD/ODEX-1 and CD/ODEX@AgNPs-2 hydrogel discs for 10 min. The SEM images of E) E. coli and F) S. aureus samples treated by (1) PBS and (2) CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogel, scale bar: 2 μm. G) Antibacterial mechanism of CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogel.

Smartphone-guided real-time AgNPs monitoring function. A) The mechanism of fluorescence internal filtering effect. B) RGB images of CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogel at different content of AgNPs under visible light and UV excitation. The R, G, B and the fitting curves of hydrogels as a function of content of AgNPs under C) ultraviolet and D) visible light are shown.

B) Smartphone-guided real-time reading and monitoring the content of AgNPs of hydrogels and corresponding RGB signals. C) The content of AgNPs variation of hydrogels.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The unpredictable release behavior of metal nanoparticles/metal ions from metal nanoparticle-loaded hydrogels, without a suitable in situ detection method, is resulting in serious cytotoxicity. To optimize the preparation and design of antibacterial hydrogels for in situ detection of metal nanoparticles, an in-situ detection platform based on the fluorescence signal change caused by the potential surface energy transfer of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) and carbon dots (CD) through silver mirror reaction and Schiff base reaction was established. The antimicrobial test results show that the composite antimicrobial hydrogel, with lower dosages of AgNPs and CD, exhibited a higher inhibition rate of 99.1 % against E. coli and 99.8 % against S. aureus compared to the single antimicrobial component. This suggests a potential synergistic antimicrobial activity. Furthermore, the fluorescence detection platform was established with a difference of <3 μg between detected values and actual values over a period of 72 h. This demonstrates the excellent in situ detection capability of the hydrogel in antimicrobial-related applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Polysaccharides such as dextran (DEX), sodium alginate, and hyaluronic acid are commonly used as hydrogel scaffolds in tissue projects due to their excellent biocompatibility and non-toxicity [1–4]. However, these polysaccharides formed scaffolds are still facing a great challenge to achieve a satisfactory inhibition effect against the growth of bacteria [5], and the misuse and overuse of antibiotics, particularly during pandemics (SARS, COVID-19, etc.), further increase the risk of mortality and morbidity during infection [6]. Meanwhile, photosensitizers [7], antimicrobial peptides (AMP) [8], quaternary ammonium [9], and hydrophobic group compounds [10,11], are commonly used as additives in hydrogels. However, they can be influenced by the complex external factors such as light, protease, pH, and ion intensity. Therefore, it is vital to fabricate functional nanoplatforms to combat bacterial infections. Inorganic antimicrobial agents, such as copper nanoparticles [12], and ZnO [13], especially, silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) have been extensively utilized as additives to prepare antimicrobial hydrogel due to their remarkable antibacterial activity [14], negligible antibacterial resistance against microorganisms [15]. For example, hydrogels that combine AgNPs modified dopamine and cationic guar gum through electrostatic interaction, and the chitosan-based hydrogels loaded with AgNPs which was reduced by UV irradiation had a significant bactericidal effect [14,16].

Although AgNPs exhibit significant antimicrobial activity, the preparation methods limit their development, including chemical reduction using reducing agents [17–19], and ultraviolet irradiation [20] which produce toxic and harmful substances such as O3 [21]. Nevertheless, the silver mirror reaction has attracted wide attention due to its mild reaction conditions, short reaction time, simple operation, and no toxic and harmful by-products [22]. Herein, the silver mirror reaction between oxidised dextran (ODEX) and silver ammonia solution was introduced, which can disperse AgNPs uniformly in the hydrophilic phase of ODEX solution without any surfactants. However, it is inadvisable for use in large doses due to the remaining cores of AgNPs having shown potential cytotoxicity. Therefore, it is crucial to monitor the release behavior of AgNPs [23]. To address the dilemma of cellular damage caused by unpredictable leakage of metal ions [24], antimicrobial hydrogels with in situ monitoring the release behavior of AgNPs was constructed. Up until now, most studies have focused on those hydrogels loading metal nanoparticles/ions [25,26], but the in situ monitoring system has seldom been reported.

For the detection of AgNPs/Ag+, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) and electrochemical analysis are complicated and time-consuming [14]. Besides, fluorescence (FL) detection has attracted more attention due to its superior sensitivity, low cost, and experimental convenience [27]. Compared to other carbon nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes [28], the carbon dots (CD) as a newly fluorescent nanomaterial have the advantages of simple preparation (microwave [29], pyrolysis [30], hydrothermal [31]), low toxicity [32], high-water solubility [33], and wide range of raw materials [34] which has been widely used to detect pH, metal ions, and other fields [35–38]. Interestingly, Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) may enhance or weaken the fluorescence emission intensity or even cause wavelength shift [39,40]. Also, AgNPs have a typical surface plasmon resonance (SPR) which involves the energy transfer between AgNPs and CD which eventually makes the fluorescence intensity change, endows the ability of in situ detections of AgNPs content [15].

In this study, CD-incorporated hydrogels with in situ monitoring of the AgNPs content antimicrobial system based on the surface energy transfer of the fluorescent CD and AgNPs were designed. AgNPs were prepared by a redox reaction of silver ammonia solution and ODEX without the involvement of any chemical reductant. The antimicrobial hydrogel with good self-healing properties constructed by silver mirror reaction and Schiff base reaction enhanced the interaction of CD and AgNPs with ODEX. Meanwhile, the synergistic antimicrobial effect of CD and AgNPs also enhanced antimicrobial activity, while also reducing the cytotoxicity caused by high doses of AgNPs. Additionally, this combination allowed for the in situ monitoring of AgNPs content through potential energy transfer between AgNPs and CD. Our work explored the key issue of the balance between cytotoxicity caused by unpredictable release of metal nanoparticles and antibacterial activity to provide a preparation strategy of drug delivery hydrogels suitable for in situ monitoring of drug release behavior.

Dextran (Mw = 70,000), and Sodium Periodate (99.5 %) were bought from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical (China). ε-Polylysine (Mw = 3500–4600, 99 %)was purchased from Adamas life (China). Silver nitrate solution with 0.10 mol/L was provided by Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology (China). Ammonia solution (25 %) was purchased from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical (China). Luria-Bertani (LB) broth medium and LB agar medium was purchased from Shanghai Titan Scientific Co., Ltd. All reagents were used directly without further purification.

CD and AgNPs were dispersed in anhydrous ethanol solution and then added dropwise to the surface of the copper mesh, and the morphology and particle size distribution were observed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, 200 kV, JEM-2100, JEOL Ltd., Japan). The optical properties of AgNPs and CD were studied with Ultraviolet–vis absorption spectra (UV-2600, wavelength: 200–700 nm) and fluorescence spectra (PerkinElmer-LS55, excitation wavelength: 305–465 nm). HRTEM with mapping was used to observe the element distribution of antibacterial hydrogel. DEX and ODEX were dispersed in deuterium hydrogen oxide for measuring nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectrum (Bruker Avance NEO 400 MHz). X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD, 10-80o) of the powder of ODEX and ODEX/AgNPs were performed with an X-ray diffraction system using Cu-Kα radiation and ODEX and ODEX/AgNPs were dispersed in deionized water for measuring zeta potential (Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90). The functional groups of the powders (CD, DEX, ODEX) and hydrogels were deduced by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet 380, average scanning number: 32, resolution: 4 cm−1) in the wavenumber range of 4000–400 cm−1. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement of freeze-dried hydrogels were carried out with a 300 W Al-Kα radiation model electron spectrometer (ESCALab 250Xi, VG Scientific) to analyze the elemental composition. The freeze-dried hydrogel was pasted on the surface of the conductive adhesive and sputter-coated with Au, then the micro-morphology of the hydrogel was observed by the scanning electron microscope (SEM, 10 kV, Hitachi S-4800 II, Japan).

The same mass of dextran and sodium periodate (3.0 g) was dissolved in 50 mL PBS solution, the mixed solution was stirred for 12 h in a dark environment at room temperature. Ethylene glycol with an equimolar molar mass of sodium periodate was added and stirred for 4 h to terminate the reaction. The obtained orange-yellow solution was removed into dialysis bag (14,000 Da) and the solution dialyzed against distilled water for 5 days, and the water was changed every 6 h. ODEX was obtained after freeze-drying, and the oxidation degree of dextran was determined by the hydroxylamine hydrochloride method [41].

CDs were synthesized following the reported method with minor modification [42]. Briefly, 1 g of ε-polylysine was calcined in a muffle furnace at 240 °C for 3 h, the resulting yellow powder was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water, sonicated for 1 h, and centrifuged (10,000 rpm for 8 min). The obtained orange-yellow supernatant was removed into dialysis bag (5000 Da) and the solution dialyzed against distilled water for 5 days, and the water was changed every 6 h. Then freeze-dried for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CD/ODEX and CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: Ammonia solution (2.5 %, w/w) was added dropwise to a shaken silver nitrate solution (0.1 mol/L) until the resulting precipitate was completely dissolved. Then silver ammonia solution (0–200 μL) was added into ODEX (10 %, w/v). Then the mixed solution was kept at a constant temperature of 50 °C for 10 min in a water bath, AgNPs/ODEX solution was obtained by silver mirror reaction [43].

The ODEX or AgNPs/ODEX solution and the CD solution (5 %, w/v) were mixed uniformly in different volume ratios. Then the mixture was poured into the mold and stood in the dark environment at room temperature to form the CD/ODEX-n and CD/ODEX@AgNPs-n hydrogels. The preparation and optimization of the various amounts of components of the CD/ODEX-n hydrogels are shown in the Table S1; The preparation and optimization of the various amounts of components of the CD/ODEX@AgNPs-n hydrogels are shown in the Table S2.

Bacterial suspensions of different concentrations were obtained by sequential dilution of the initial bacterial suspension with a dilution factor of 10. Each diluted bacterial suspension was cultured using the dilution spread plate method. The concentration of bacteria was determined using the appropriate method (see Supporting Information). The CD/ODEX and CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogel were prepared as a disc with a diameter of 1 cm and a thickness of 2 mm and transferred to an LB agar plate covered with 100 μL of 107 CFU/mL fresh bacterial suspension. After incubating at 37 °C overnight, the bacterial inhibition zone was measured. Each experiment was repeated three times. Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) SHBCC D10842 and Escherichia coli (E. coli) ATCC 35218 purchased from Shanghai Bioresource Collection Center were chosen as the model pathogen for antibacterial activity experiments. Before the experiments, the purity of S. aureus and E. coli is determined by morphological characteristics and microscopic observation.

CD/ODEX and CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogels were prepared in 12-well plates, respectively. When the gelation is completed, 20 μL of 107 CFU/mL fresh S. aureus suspension was added to each hydrogel surface. Antibacterial tests were performed according to El-Hadary [44]. The 12-well plates were placed in the bacterial incubator for 10 min, 1 mL of sterile PBS was added to each well and gently sonicated to resuspend the surviving bacteria, 100 μL of the resuspended bacterial suspension from each well was spread on the surface of the culture medium and incubated at 37 °C for 16 h, the colony counts on the plates were recorded. The control group consisted of 20 μL of 107 CFU/mL fresh bacterial suspension without hydrogel. For antimicrobial testing of individual components, ODEX (8 μg/mL), CD (8 μg/mL), AgNO3 (Ag+: 8 μg/mL), ODEX/AgNPs (AgNPs: 8 μg/mL) were used for the same antimicrobial test method. The bacterial survival assay was repeated three times.

According to the Gao's method, hydrogels with different content of AgNPs were placed in a self-made fluorescence detection device to detect the fluorescence images [35]. Then the images were decomposed into RGB signals to obtain the relationship between the corresponding color values and the content of AgNPs based on Li's method [38]. Standard curves were analyzed from the data.

The CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogels (silver ammonia solution of 10 μL was added) were prepared as a disc with a diameter of 1 cm and a thickness of 2 mm and transferred to an LB agar plate covered with 100 μL of 107 CFU/mL fresh bacterial suspension. After incubating at 37 °C for 0 h, 12 h, 24 h, 36 h, 48 h, 60 h, and 72 h, the content of AgNPs was recorded by a self-made fluorescence detector.

The detection process of UV light excitation was carried out in a dark environment to avoid the interference of other light sources: the hydrogel emits blue fluorescence under UV light excitation, the image of hydrogels was taken by a smartphone, then the color processing software was used to decompose the color of image into RGB signals. At last, the corresponding G and B values was used to calculate the content of AgNPs with the Eq. (1).
(1)
Content
AgNPs , μg
= − 40.29 ln
0.22 GB − 789.17
+ 365.22

The detection process of sunlight excitation was carried out in sunlight environment: the image of hydrogels under sunlight was taken by a smartphone, then the color processing software was used to decompose the color of image into RGB signals. At last, the corresponding G and B values was used to calculate the content of AgNPs with the Eq. (2).
(2)
Content
AgNPs , μg
= 25.78 ln
1.68 G / B − 1.92
+ 3.26

Meanwhile, the inductively coupled plasma tester (ICP-MS, Optima 8000, PerkinElmer, USA) was used to quantify the remaining amount of Ag element of the hydrogel. All photos were taken via the smartphone. Each experiment was repeated three times.

According to previous studies [45], the cytotoxicity tests of hydrogels were conducted using HeLa cell which provided by Shanghai Quicell Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (China). Briefly, 1 g CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogel was rinsed with 5 mL complete MEM medium and soaked in 15 mL medium for 24 h in an incubator. The relative concentration of the hydrogel extract (1 g/15 mL) is defined as 100 %. 100 μL of HeLa cell suspension (passage number: 7) was added to a 96-well plate (5 × 103 cells per well) and incubated at 37 °C under 5 % CO2. Cells on the 96-well plate without hydrogel were used as control. The cells were then incubated for 48 h and 96 h. The culture medium was removed. Each well was washed by PBS three times and 100 μL of 0.5 mg/mL MTT was added, further incubated in the incubator for 4 h. The supernatant was removed and 100 μL DMSO was added, shaked gently for 10 min, and measured for the absorption at 490 nm. A Live/Dead Viability/Cytotoxicity kit (Molecular Probe) was used to stain the HeLa cells. First, the cells were incubated in PBS containing 2 μM Calcein AM and 1 μM ethidium homodimer-1 (EthD-1) for 30 min in a 96-well cell culture plate. Live cells were stained with Calcein AM (green), and dead cells were stained with EthD-1 (red). The cells were then incubated for 48 h and 96 h and imaged for morphology with a fluorescence microscope.

All experiments described above were repeated in triplicate and tested for homogeneity of variance according to El-Hadary et al. [44]. Parametric datasets were analyzed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) followed by Tukey's HSD post hoc test. Parison. Data are reported as mean ± S.D. Significant difference is defined as P < 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of AgNPs and CD
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, AgNPs and CD were integrated into the hydrogel network using a one-step oxidation-reduction reaction and Schiff base reaction, as illustrated in Fig. 1 . The synergistic effect of AgNPs and CDs, along with surface energy transfer, significantly enhanced the antibacterial activity and provided the ability to monitor the AgNPs content in situ. Compared to other antimicrobial hydrogels, we chose to use the silver mirror reaction to rapidly prepare hydrogels loaded with silver nanoparticles, thereby avoiding the use of chemical reducing agents (Table S5) [14,46]. As shown in Fig. S1A, within 10 min, the absorption peak corresponding to AgNPs at 420 nm gradually increased and remained constant, reflecting the progress of the reaction between the aldehyde group and the silver ammonia solution [21]. Meanwhile, the absorption peak at 239 nm corresponds to the aldehyde/carboxyl of ODEX, indicating that the hydroxyl groups of the oxidised dextran continuously react with the remaining Ag+, leading to changes in the absorption peak (Fig. S1B). The quasi-spherical AgNPs have an average particle size of 36.32 nm, and it has been reported that AgNPs of this size exhibit stronger antimicrobial activity compared to larger AgNPs [17]. As shown in Fig. 2A and D, the 0.24 nm lattice fringes correspond to the 111 reflective planes of the AgNPs. To provide the antimicrobial hydrogel with good antimicrobial properties and monitoring capabilities, CD with 4.92 nm were obtained by one-pot calcination of ε-polylysine (Fig. 2B and E) [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of CD/ODEX and CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: In addition, CD/ODEX and CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogels were obtained through the Schiff base reaction. In the hydrogels, the stability and dispersity of AgNPs and CD are jointly enhanced by the combined effects of Schiff base linkages, hydrogen bonds, and electrostatic attractions. SEM images of CD/ODEX and CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogels are shown in Fig. 2C and F, respectively, demonstrating a uniform pore size distribution, with average pore diameters of 98.56 μm and 61.43 μm, respectively. The porous structure of these hydrogels is beneficial for the release of AgNPs and Ag+.

For the CD/ODEX hydrogels, as the CD ratio increased from 1:2 to 3:1 (v/v, Table S1), the CD/ODEX-1 hydrogel exhibited a pore structure with relatively uniform pore sizes (98.56 μm). For CD/ODEX-0.5, due to the intermolecular hydrogen bonding at low CD content, the long chains of ODEX tend to curl and aggregate, resulting in adhesion between pore structures. For CD/ODEX-2 and CD/ODEX-3, higher CD contents tend to self-aggregate, which affects the formation of the cross-linking network and pore structure. The hydrogels exhibited adhesive properties and had irregular pore structures. The gelation time first decreased and then increased, demonstrating the influence of the ratio of amino to aldehyde groups on the Schiff base formation process (Figs. S5 and S6). The CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogels (Table S2) undergo a silver mirror reaction that converts some aldehyde groups to carboxyl groups. These carboxyl groups then bind to the amino groups of CD through electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding, resulting in a non-covalent cross-linked structure of the hydrogel network. The hydrogel experiences covalent and non-covalent interactions, leading to a reduced gelation time and smaller pore size. The average pore diameter of the CD/ODEX@AgNPs-3 hydrogel is 61.43 μm. As the amount of silver ammonia solution added is further increased, both gelation time and pore size increase (Figs. S7–9), indicating that an excess of aldehyde groups is converted into carboxyl groups. Fig. S10 shows the swelling ratio of CD/ODEX@AgNPs-n (n = 0, 3, 7), indicating greater solution absorption with larger pore sizes. The cross-linking network of the hydrogel gradually shifts from Schiff bases to weaker electrostatic interactions with carboxyl and amino groups, resulting in a significant increase in both gelation time and pore size. Eventually, the system fails to form a hydrogel when most of the aldehyde groups are converted to carboxyl groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface energy transfer between CD and AgNPs
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of ODEX was further confirmed by nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) analysis (Fig. S3) [47]. The degree of oxidation of dextran was determined to be 30.4 % by the hydroxylamine hydrochloride method [41]. The optical properties of CD and AgNPs were analyzed using fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 2G) and ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectroscopy (Fig. 2H, I). The UV–vis spectra show that CD has two characteristic absorption peaks, attributed to CC (235 nm) and CO (345 nm) bonds, respectively [48]. The inset in Fig. 2H demonstrates that a low concentration CD solution appears colorless and transparent in daylight but appears a bright blue color when excited by UV light at 365 nm.

Simultaneously, the fluorescence emission spectrum was tested further by exploiting the excitation wavelength dependence of CD (Fig. 2G). Interestingly, AgNPs exhibited a strong absorption peak around 420 nm (half-width: 370–460 nm), which is attributed to the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect. The fluorescence signal changes can be analyzed to determine the content of AgNPs with the aid of FRET and the SPR effect, indicating a potential surface energy transfer between CD and AgNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CD/ODEX and CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: One step further, XRD and zeta potential tests were conducted to demonstrate the silver mirror reaction between AgNPs and ODEX. Fig. 3A shows that, ODEX@AgNPs show new peaks at 38.1°, 44.3°, 64.4°, and 77.5°, corresponding to Ag (111), Ag (200), Ag (220) and Ag (311) respectively (Fig. 2D) [26]. This indicates the successful preparation of AgNPs after the silver mirror reaction. Meanwhile, the zeta potential change indicates successful oxidation of the aldehyde group of ODEX was successfully oxidised to a carboxyl group, resulting in a slight decrease in potential from −3.08 mV to −8.05 mV. Also, the zeta potential of CD and CD/ODEX/AgNPs is 15.3 mV and 4.19 mV, respectively (Fig. S11). This indicates that the hydrogels have electrostatic interactions with negatively charged bacterial cell membranes. In conclusion, the ODEX/AgNPs precursors were successfully synthesized by the silver mirror reaction.

To confirm the successful surface modification of the precursor and hydrogels, FT-IR was performed (Fig. 3C). In contrast to the initial DEX, ODEX showed a new peak at 1730 cm−1, which can be attributed to the stretching vibration of the CO group of the aldehyde groups from ODEX. Additionally, a peak appeared at 1650 cm−1, which can be attributed to the stretching vibration of the CO group of a carboxyl group from ODEX (Fig. S2) [49]. It was demonstrated that a part of the hydroxyl groups on the surface of DEX were converted to aldehyde groups and further converted to carboxyl groups after the silver mirror reaction in the ODEX/AgNPs precursor. It is noteworthy that a distinct peak at ca. 3450 cm−1, which could be attributed to the NH group derived from ε-polylysine [50]. After mixing CD and precursor solutions, the CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogel shows a strong band at ca. 1676 cm−1, indicating the formation of Schiff base bonds between the amino groups from CD and aldehyde groups from ODEX.

The full X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogel contains characterized peaks at 284.6 eV for C 1 s, 399.3 eV for N 1 s, 532.1 eV for O 1 s, and 367.9 eV for Ag 3d, as shown in Fig. 2D. The C 1 s band has three component peaks at 284.6 eV, 285.7 eV, and 287.3 eV corresponding to C-C/C=C, C-O/C-N, and C=O/C=N, respectively. The CC bonds of CD are also attributed to the alkane chain in polylysine (Fig. S4), which has typical amino (−NH2) and carboxyl (CO, CO) groups. The CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogel had a higher CO content than the CD/ODEX hydrogel, as shown in Figs. 3E, F, and S4. It is speculated that the silver mirror reaction converted the aldehyde groups into carboxyl groups, reducing the CH bond content and increasing the CO bond content. A similar situation appears in Fig. 3G, the peaks at 399.3 eV, 400.2 eV, and 401.4 eV of the N 1 s band were attributed to -NH/-N, -C=N, and -NH2 from CD, respectively [30]. The formation of the Schiff base bond resulted in the consumption of part of the aldehyde group, leading to a decrease in the CN content. Simultaneously, AgNPs were formed. Fig. 3H shows that the Ag 3d3/2 and Ag 3d5/2 peaks at 372.4 eV and 366.4 eV can be assigned to AgNPs, those at 373.6 eV and 367.2 eV are attributed to Ag+. The XPS results agree with the FTIR analysis of the CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogel. AgNPs are generated by the redox reaction between ODEX and silver ammonia solution. The reaction process is affected by temperature and viscosity, and Ag+ is not completely converted to AgNPs. XPS analysis reveals that AgNPs and Ag+ still coexist within the polymer network. Fig. 3I–L demonstrates the uniform dispersion of the elements C, N, O and Ag through elemental mapping.

AgNPs and CDs can act as antimicrobial agents, eliminating bacterial infections and providing long-lasting antimicrobial activity. The antimicrobial efficacy of ODEX, CDs, and ODEX/AgNPs was further evaluated by antibacterial tests. Fig. 4A shows that ODEX has almost no antibacterial activity. Meanwhile, the minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs) of ODEX/AgNPs and CDs are 4 μg/mL and 32 μg/mL, respectively (Table S4). The lower MIC of ODEX/AgNPs is attributed to the release of AgNPs that accumulate on the bacterial surface due to the strong affinity of the bacterial surface glycoprotein [6], as well as the physical damage to DNA and proteins in the cells caused by Ag+ [51].

Further, CD/ODEX and CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogel were used against E. coli and S. aureus. An apparent inhibitory zone in Fig. 4B was observed, indicating that CD/ODEX@AgNPs (15.2 ± 1.8 mm against E. coli and 14.8 ± 1.2 mm against S. aureus) is slightly smaller than CD/ODEX (16.1 ± 1.5 mm against E. coli and 15.5 ± 1.2 mm against S. aureus) hydrogel. This suggests that electrostatic interaction can further strengthen the network structure of the hydrogel, anchor AgNPs and CD in the hydrogel network, and reduce the leaching of antibacterial substances, as shown in Fig. 4C and D. Within the slightly acidic environment of a bacterial infection, the coordination effect of carboxyl and AgNPs/Ag+ decreases, causing natural leaching. Moreover, the superior antimicrobial property of CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogel was further demonstrated by the direct contact test in Fig. 4B. The results demonstrate that the CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogel effectively inhibits bacterial growth, as shown in Fig. 4D. Additionally, the reduction in the dosage of AgNPs and CD suggests a potential synergistic antibacterial effect, as indicated in Table S4.

It is worth noting that AgNPs can react with hydrogen peroxide to produce hydroxyl radicals under mildly acidic conditions [52], which are over-expressed in the microenvironment of bacterial infections. Their intense oxidative stress enables the destruction of important biological entities like enzymes and DNA [53]. The protonated CD, which have positive amino groups. Are beneficial for accumulating on the surface of negatively charged bacteria [54]. This affects the fluidity of the cell membrane, changing its permeability and making it easier for antibacterial substances (AgNPs/Ag+) to enter the cell. Finally, antibacterial substances destroy DNA and inactivate proteins, leading to the efflux of bacterial contents, resulting in bacterial death (Fig. 4E, F) [55,56], as shown in Figs. 4G and S14. Further cytotoxicity experiments on the hydrogel were performed, and Fig. S13 demonstrates that cells in the hydrogel group maintained over 95 % viability compared to the control group that was not co-cultured with the hydrogel. This indicates that the CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogels have good biocompatibility, making it suitable for biological applications such as wound dressings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ monitoring system of CD/ODEX@AgNP hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogels with detection capabilities are generally complex to prepare and functionally monolithic. Their advancement is hindered by pollution caused by microelectronic contact patterns and the inaccuracies of visual colorimetric detection (Table S5) [38,42]. Surface energy transfer based on CD and AgNPs not only possesses excellent antibacterial activity but also enables in-situ monitoring of AgNPs release behavior in CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogels. This expands the potential applications of hydrogels in metal detection and antibacterial-related fields.

Consequently, smartphones were used to collect diagnostic data from CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogels, thereby developing a non-contact in situ detection technology (Fig. 5A). As shown in Fig. 5B, The fluorescence signal was converted into red (R), green (G), and blue (B) signals, improving the accuracy of dual-color measurement and addressing the fragility and susceptibility to interference of microelectronic monitoring systems [38]. Within the hydrogel, the RGB signals of AgNPs and their corresponding concentrations are further analyzed by RGB data (Fig. 5C and D). Additionally, the fluorescence intensity of CD remained stable during antibacterial experiments and upon the addition of silver ions, as shown in Fig. S15. It is important to note that the bicolorimetric monitoring system is not only highly sensitive but also environmentally friendly (non-pollutant).

Under ultraviolet excitation, the R value of the CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogel remains constant, while the G and B values gradually decrease. The signal is normalized by multiplying each G value by its corresponding B value. It is noteworthy that the corresponding Eq. (1) was obtained through fitting analysis of the relationship between G*B (GB) and the content of AgNPs (Fig. 5D).
(1)
Content
AgNPs,
μg
= − 40.29 ln
0 . 22 GB - 789 . 17
+ 365 . 22

Furthermore, under visible light, as the content of AgNPs increases, the R, G, B values of the CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogel will gradually change with the increase of AgNPs content (see Fig. 5C). The corresponding Eq. (2) can also be derived:
(2)
Content
AgNPs , μg
= 25.78 ln
1.68 G / B − 1.92
+ 3.26

Since the hydrogel's actual color is easily influenced by ambient light, visual judgment results are inaccurate. Therefore, the relative changes in the hydrogel under visible light are not discussed here. The CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogel was co-cultured with bacteria for 0 h, 12 h, 24 h, 36 h, 48 h, 60 h, and 72 h (Fig. 6A). The discussion focuses on changes in fluorescence signals, and the theoretical content of AgNPs is calculated using Eq. (1) (Fig. 6B).

Here, we investigate the in situ detection capabilities of hydrogels during the antimicrobial processes using ICP-MS, as shown in Fig. 6. The migration of AgNPs within the polymer framework is accompanied by the dissolution of Ag+ [57]. Ag+ is more likely to migrate from the intricate three-dimensional network of hydrogels to the external solutions compared to AgNPs. Interestingly, TEM revealed the presence of AgNPs in the external solution during the hydrogel degradation process (Fig. S12B). Additionally, XPS analysis (Fig. S12A) was conducted on the silver elements in the hydrogel, revealing the presence of a small amount of Ag+ [14]. This suggests that the reduction of AgNPs in the hydrogel may be attributed to the simultaneous migration or release of both AgNPs and Ag+.

It can be seen that at the beginning of the test, the actual Ag content was slightly higher than the calculated value (Fig. 6C), indicating the presence of residual Ag+ that did not participate in the silver mirror reaction. With the increase of time, partial degradation of the hydrogel leads to the leaching of CD, which weakens the fluorescence signal, resulting in larger calculated values. Furthermore, as the time increased, more AgNPs were converted to Ag+, further enhancing the fluorescence signals and resulting in smaller calculated values smaller. Nevertheless, the deviation between the test results (ICP-MS) and the calculated results is <3 μg. The results show a relatively similar degree of agreement, and the handmade detector assembled with a smartphone can be used for fast and convenient in situ detection of changes in the content of AgNPs in hydrogels compared to contact mode [14]. Fig. 6C shows that the experimental tests and the ICP-MS tests yield similar results, but there is still a certain degree of error, and variations in testing equipment and experimental conditions may affect the accuracy of the monitoring results.

In summary, this work presents a paradigm for CD-based nanomaterials antimicrobial platform with a strong synergistic effect achieved by loading AgNPs into the antibacterial hydrogel. The CD/ODEX@AgNPs hydrogel enables in situ monitoring of the AgNPs content through surface energy transfer in hydrogels. Although this work provides an in situ monitoring concept for the release of metal nanoparticles, it may be greatly influenced by the device and practical conditions. The follow-up work will further study the real-time monitoring of metal nanoparticles using professional signal-receiving processor devices. The entire fabrication process requires only a simple reaction, minimal set-up, and mild experimental conditions. This offers a straightforward way towards smart and robust antibacterial materials through the facile integration of low-cost and abundant polysaccharides. The nanoplatform demonstrates excellent ability to monitor the release behavior of AgNPs in situ and exhibits synergistic antibacterial effects. The hydrogel, which possesses superior biocompatibility and degradability, has profound potential for antibacterial applications. The research presents a new strategy for preparing Schiff base hydrogels capable of detecting AgNPs in situ without involving chemical reductants. Optimizing the structural design of drug-carrying hydrogels is expected to overcome the challenge of simultaneously achieving hydrogels with excellent antimicrobial activity and biosafety.
